definition_origin: Mascot animation is the process of bringing a static character bible (see Item 18) to life across the app's emotional states — idle breathing, celebrations, sad reactions, interactive eye-tracking. Three production paradigms exist: (1) Lottie — vector keyframe animation, exported from After Effects/Lottielab/Rive; (2) Rive — vector + state machine + scripting + data binding, runtime-interactive; (3) Frame-by-frame — sprite sheets, GIF, WebM, classic flipbook. Each has distinct trade-offs in interactivity, file size, runtime cost.
core_purpose: Static mascots are flat brand. Animated mascots are relationships — Visceral (the breathing idle creates a sense of presence on the home screen), Behavioral (the mascot reacts to your action: celebration on streak save, sad-puppy-eyes on error), Reflective (users name the mascot, screenshot reactions, share — it becomes IP). The state-machine mascot in particular collapses 'animation as decoration' into 'animation as UI logic' — the mascot literally is the loading state, the success confirmation, the empty state.
when_needed: Same as Item 18: heavy in consumer mobile (Duolingo, Finch, Habitica), AI-wrappers (Replika, Pi.ai for avatars), wellness, learning, kids. Sparingly in B2B/dev-tool. State-machine animation specifically required when the mascot needs to react to user input or app state — onboarding flows where the mascot reacts to your name input, AI-chat with avatar that lip-syncs to streaming output. Static or simple-loop Lottie sufficient when mascot is decorative.
format_options: Lottie JSON / dotLottie: timeline-based animations, 10-50KB per state, ideal for non-interactive loops and tap-triggered playback. Best authoring: After Effects + LottieFiles plugin, OR Lottielab (no AE), OR Rive's Lottie export. Rive .riv: state machines + scripting + data binding + multi-artboard, 1-30KB per file (10-15x smaller than equivalent Lottie). Best authoring: Rive web/desktop editor (free). Required when: mascot reacts to user input (eye-tracking, hover, scroll progress, AI-streaming progress, real-time data). Frame-by-frame sprite atlas (PNG): legacy, 50KB-2MB depending on frames, only when vector won't capture the style (e.g. pixel-art Habitica, painterly hand-drawn). WebP/AVIF animated: smaller than GIF, decent fallback, no interactivity. APNG: lossless animated PNG, niche. WebM/MP4 with alpha: heaviest but maximum visual fidelity (Pixar-style 3D mascot videos) — only for marketing. Recommendation hierarchy: Rive > dotLottie > sprite-sheet > video.
file_size_performance: Lottie state: 5-30 KB ideal, ≤50 KB tolerable, >100 KB requires audit. dotLottie multi-state bundle: pack 8-12 states into one ≤80 KB file. Rive: typically 5-25 KB total per character with full state machine — 10-15x smaller than equivalent Lottie. Sprite atlas: 200KB-1MB for 30-60 frame loop at 2x retina. Runtime cost: Lottie ~3-5% CPU on mid-tier mobile while playing; Rive 1-2%; sprite atlas highest (texture upload). Battery: state-machine mascot that idles continuously is more expensive than tap-triggered Lottie — pause when app is backgrounded (use AppState in RN). Memory: Rive shares texture atlas across artboards — most efficient for mascots with many states.
free_tools: Rive Editor (free for individuals): web + desktop, full state machine + listeners + data binding, exports .riv + Lottie + GIF + video. Best free path for interactive mascots. Lottielab (free tier): web-based Lottie authoring, no AE required, character animation supported via parented layers, free for public projects. LottieFiles AE plugin (free, requires AE — paid). Bodymovin (free, AE only). Cascadeur (free for non-commercial; Indie $8/mo for commercial up to $100k revenue): AI-assisted 3D character animation, exports FBX/glTF. ComfyUI + AnimateDiff (free local, GPU required): AI-driven 2D character motion from static reference + prompt; useful for generating reference videos to trace as Lottie. Wonder Dynamics free trial (motion capture from video). Cavalry (free for individuals): node-based motion design, can drive procedural mascot motion. Procreate Dreams (paid one-time $19.99 iPad — borderline): frame-by-frame on iPad, simplest no-skill option. Krita (free): frame-by-frame digital painting + animation. OpenToonz (free FOSS): Studio Ghibli's tool, frame-by-frame and bone rigging. Pencil2D (free): simple frame-by-frame. Spine free trial (paid, but trial to evaluate). Motion Bank (free 14-day trial of pose libraries — note: 'Motion Bank' here refers to motion-pose reference databases, not a single tool).
paid_tools: Adobe After Effects ($22.99-59.99/mo): the ceiling for Lottie character animation — every expression, every effect. Worth it when shipping >5 character animations/month. Rive Pro ($16-32/seat/mo): adds team workspace, version history, advanced runtime; the free tier handles most solo-dev needs. Spine ($69 one-time Essential, $329 Professional): industry-standard 2D skeletal mesh animation for games, exports to Lottie via plugins; only worth it for game-quality character rigs. Cascadeur Indie ($8/mo annual): commercial license, FBX/glTF export — cheapest professional 3D character animation. Adobe Character Animator (included with CC): face-tracking puppet animation, niche but useful for talking-head mascot reels. Tiering: starter $8-16/mo (Cascadeur Indie OR Rive Pro) — sufficient for solo dev. Pro $20-30/mo (After Effects standalone) — for committed Lottie character work. Enterprise $50+/mo (Adobe CC + Rive Pro + Spine) — multi-creator teams.
gemini_pro_workflow: Step 1 — Define motion personality with Gemini Pro: 'My mascot is {character} with archetype {archetype}. Suggest a motion-personality matrix: idle-breathing rhythm, celebration timing, sad-deflate timing, surprise-jump timing. Output ms timing, easing curves (cubic-bezier or spring stiffness/damping/mass), and 12-principles applied per state.' Step 2 — Veo for reference videos: prompt 'Friendly cartoon owl waving its wings, looped, 2 seconds, side-lit, neutral background' — use the AI-generated reference as motion-intent NOT as final asset. Step 3 — Imagen 4 + Whisk to generate the keyframe stills (initial pose, mid pose, peak pose, return pose) with locked character bible reference. Step 4 — Import keyframe stills into Lottielab or Rive as PNG references; recreate as vector keyframes. Step 5 — Gemini Pro spring-tuning prompt: 'For a {action} animation on a {archetype} mascot, suggest spring physics (stiffness, damping, mass, initial velocity). Provide 3 variants: snappy, bouncy, settled.' Step 6 — Critique pass: paste exported Lottie/Rive playback video + ask Gemini multimodal 'Identify motion problems: missing anticipation, no follow-through, broken arc, ease-in not ease-out, palette flicker.' Custom Gem: 'MotionDirector' Gem, system prompt 'You are a senior motion designer. For each animation state requested, output: keyframe table (time/transform/easing per layer), Disney 12-principles applied, and target ms duration.'
workflow_steps: 1. Pick paradigm (15 min): Rive if interactive/state-driven; Lottie if linear/looping; sprite if vector won't carry the style. 2. Pull from character bible (10 min): import canonical poses + expression references. 3. Rig (60-120 min): Lottie — parent layers (head→body→arm-shoulder→arm-forearm→arm-hand) with anchor points at joints. Rive — bones + meshes + constraints. 4. Block keyframes (45 min): for each state, set 3-5 major keyframes (start, anticipation, peak, follow-through, settle). 5. Spacing pass (30 min): adjust between-key timing — fast acceleration then slow settle, or hang-time at peak. 6. Easing pass (20 min): replace defaults with spring or custom Bezier; cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) for friendly bounce, (0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) for premium ease-out. 7. Secondary motion (45 min): tail wag, ear flop, hair, antennae — offset 3-5 frames behind primary. 8. State machine (Rive only, 30-60 min): connect states with transitions, listeners (onPointerEnter, onPointerExit, onClick), inputs (mood: number 0-100). 9. Optimize (20 min): drop unused layers, target ≤30KB Rive / ≤50KB Lottie. 10. Export + integrate (20 min): rive-react / lottie-react / lottie-react-native. 11. Reduced-motion fallback (15 min): static expression PNG. 12. Test on low-end Android. Total time-to-first-mascot-animation for solo dev: Lottie loop ~4-6 hr first time; Rive state machine ~6-10 hr first time; subsequent ~1-2 hr each.
ai_prompt_templates: Motion-spec prompt (Gemini): 'For mascot {character_archetype}, generate a motion bible: idle (breathing rhythm in ms, scale variance, easing), celebration (jump height %, anticipation frames, follow-through frames, total duration ms), sad (deflate timing, droop angle, hold ms), surprise (jump-back distance, eye-pop scale, settle ms). Apply Disney 12-principles. Output JSON.' Veo reference prompt: 'Animated {character} {action}, soft motion, looped 2 seconds, neutral background, side-lit, single-character on plain background, behance editorial style mascot animation reference.' Spring-tuning prompt: 'For a {action} on a {archetype} mascot, recommend spring physics (stiffness 50-500, damping 5-50, mass 0.5-3) for: snappy / bouncy / settled / heavy variants. Show resulting overshoot percentage and settle time ms.' Critique prompt (Gemini multimodal, paste video): 'Critique this mascot animation for Disney 12-principles violations. Score 0-10 on: anticipation, follow-through, slow-in/slow-out, arcs, secondary action, exaggeration, timing, appeal. List 3 specific frame-level fixes.' AnimateDiff (ComfyUI) prompt for reference video: '{character_bible_description}, {action}, side view, neutral background, 24fps, 32 frames, smooth motion, behance editorial mascot, no melting, no glitches, exact palette {hex1} {hex2}.'
claude_code_integration: Install: pnpm add @rive-app/react-canvas (web/RN) OR rive-react-native, lottie-react-native, @lottiefiles/dotlottie-react. Asset paths: /apps/mobile/src/assets/mascot/{state}.{riv,lottie,json}. Claude Code can: (1) generate <Mascot state="happy" inputs={{ mood: 80 }} /> typed wrapper that switches between Rive state-machine inputs and Lottie playback by asset type; (2) write rive-runtime initialization with reduced-motion gate and AppState pause-on-background; (3) emit a state-map type (StateMap = 'idle' | 'happy' | 'sad' | ...) by parsing the .riv state machine; (4) generate Storybook stories iterating every state; (5) write CI script that benchmarks each animation's CPU/memory cost on a Pixel-3 emulator; (6) generate prefers-reduced-motion fallback static PNG/SVG by extracting the first frame; (7) wire a debug overlay component showing current state + transition timeline.
emotional_design_integration: Caregiver: slow ease-in-out, breathing rhythm 1.2-1.5s, gentle bounce, never-jarring transitions, soft palette retention through motion. Magician: morph transitions between states (no hard cuts), particle trails, glow on action, shape-shift mascot. Jester: rubber-band overshoot (cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1)), squash-and-stretch exaggeration, expressive face, fast 200-400ms transitions. Hero: bold confident timing, anticipation pause + powerful follow-through, triumphant settle. Innocent: simple linear-ish motion, minimal secondary motion, child-like predictability. Sage: minimal motion, precise timing, no excess — sometimes the right Sage mascot is a single eye-blink and nothing else. The brand-motion personality must match across all mascot states; one Magician state mixed with Jester eases reads as broken.
specific_examples: Duolingo's Duo (https://www.duolingo.com) — Lottie-based across iOS/Android/web, hundreds of states, Lottie powers push-notification animations. Finch's bird mascot — Rive state machine reacts to in-app actions (feeding, walking, sleeping). Headspace's color-blob — Lottie breathing meditation companions. Replika avatar — 3D state machine, real-time facial reactions to chat sentiment. Pi.ai abstract orb — pulses/morphs to mark thinking/streaming/complete states (Magician archetype). Slackbot's emoji reactions — restrained Lottie celebrations. GitHub's loading Octocat — Lottie. Rive showcase (https://rive.app/community/files) — many production examples. Discord Wumpus — Lottie + sprite hybrid. Apple's Memoji — proprietary 3D state-machine system. Rive's own Lottie-alternative blog (https://rive.app/blog/rive-as-a-lottie-alternative) — case studies of brands migrating. Animaj's Pocoyo IP — full character animation pipeline.
anti_ai_slop_traps: (1) Linear easing on character motion — instantly reads as cheap; counter: spring or cubic-bezier with overshoot. (2) Missing anticipation — character pops to action without a wind-up; counter: always 2-4 frames of prep before main action. (3) No follow-through — character hard-stops at end pose; counter: 3-5 frames of overshoot + settle. (4) All limbs animate in lock-step — robotic; counter: offset secondary motion 3-5 frames behind primary. (5) AI-generated mascot video (Veo/Sora-style) used as final asset — usually has temporal artifacts (flickering palette, melting features); counter: use only as reference, redraw as vector. (6) AnimateDiff outputs with frame-to-frame jitter — unusable as-is; counter: use as keyframe inspiration only. (7) Eye-pupils that don't track motion — pupils should anticipate where the head is going. (8) Idle that loops without breath/microsway — character looks dead; counter: always include 2-3% scale or 1-2px Y oscillation. (9) Eye-blinks at perfectly even intervals — reads as mechanical; counter: vary intervals 1.5-4s with occasional double-blink. (10) Same easing curve on every state — counter: define motion personality per state (snappy celebration vs settled sad). Counter-prompts for AI animation reference: 'NO temporal flicker, NO palette drift, NO melting features, smooth motion arcs, consistent character bible, behance editorial mascot animation reference, NOT for direct production use.'
common_pitfalls: Performance: stacking idle-loop Rive + idle-loop Lottie + screen animations crushes low-end Android — use AppState/visibility to pause off-screen. Memory leaks: not destroying Rive instances on unmount — use rive-react's cleanup hooks. State-machine sprawl: 50 states with no taxonomy — group into state-categories (mood, action, scene-context). Rig brittleness: anchor points off → motion looks wrong; always set anchor at joint. Layer naming: 'Layer 1, Layer 2' instead of 'head, eye-l, mouth' — makes runtime control impossible. Color hard-coding: can't theme — use Rive runtime properties or Lottie color override. Audio sync: assuming Lottie carries audio (it doesn't — pair separately). Reduced-motion: unconditionally autoplaying loops triggers vestibular issues. Scope creep: 40 states when 8 are sufficient. Tooling lock-in: Rive's hosted editor — keep .riv source files in git. Frame-rate assumption: Lottie defaults to 30fps; iOS ProMotion supports 120fps but most Lotties don't have keyframes for it — design at 60fps for safe headroom.
accessibility_notes: Always honor prefers-reduced-motion: render static expression PNG. Provide aria-label per state ('mascot celebrating', 'mascot thinking'). Pair animation with text/icon for state communication (don't rely on motion alone for meaning). Avoid >3Hz flashing (WCAG 2.3.1). Provide a global motion-toggle in app settings. For looping decorative idles, mark aria-hidden=true. Pause animations when not in viewport.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile (RN): rive-react-native and lottie-react-native both mature; Rive runtime is C++ with tighter perf, Lottie uses native renderers (CALayer/Drawable) with renderer-version skew. Test both iOS/Android. dotLottie support requires lottie-react-native v6.7+. Web: rive-app/react-canvas + @lottiefiles/dotlottie-react; lazy-load via dynamic import; can ship larger bundles. Native iOS: lottie-ios is most polished native runtime; Rive iOS native (Cocoa) also solid. ProMotion (120fps): Lottie keyframes need to be authored at 60+fps to feel smooth; Rive scales naturally via its runtime.
sources: [Rive vs Lottie 2026 (Rive Masterclass)](https://www.rivemasterclass.com/blog/rive-vs-lottie); [Stop Using Lottie for Characters (Dev.to)](https://dev.to/uianimation/stop-using-lottie-for-characters-why-rive-is-the-future-of-app-animation-1hjf); [Building Interactive Mascots in Flutter with Rive 2026 (Dev.to)](https://dev.to/uianimation/building-high-performance-interactive-mascots-in-flutter-with-rive-production-guide-for-2026-17c6); [Rive vs Lottie 2026: Logic + Data (Rive Masterclass)](https://www.rivemasterclass.com/blog/rive-vs-lottie-in-20260why-interactive-logic-data-binding-scripting-make-rive-the-future-of-ui-animation); [Rive as Lottie Alternative (official)](https://rive.app/blog/rive-as-a-lottie-alternative); [Cascadeur 2026.1 release (CGChannel)](https://www.cgchannel.com/2026/04/nekki-releases-cascadeur-2026-1/); [Cascadeur pricing](https://cascadeur.com/plans); [ComfyUI AnimateDiff Evolved GitHub](https://github.com/Kosinkadink/ComfyUI-AnimateDiff-Evolved); [Lottie vs Rive (Motion Agency)](https://www.motiontheagency.com/blog/lottie-vs-rive)
